title: Lesson 213
transcript: One of the most important aspects of security management is what we call governance and specifically we're going to be talking about security governance and how it should align with corporate governance, but before we can get into how we align the two, let's define the word governance itself. And so if we write this, we have the word governance. Now, what does this mean? In fact, the word itself, if you spit it up, comes from the verb to govern. And what does that mean? Obviously, we elect officials, for example, as part of government. When you go and vote for someone, We're voting for a government to ultimately represent us. The whole purpose of that government and those people that make up government is to govern the country, for example, in such a way so that it gains in value and it gains in the value of the assets that are part of that country like for example its people. And so effective governance is always focused on increasing value like we've been talking about this entire course. And so in a corporate environment, we have the same thing. We have people that are going to initiate and activate and drive what the organization does in order for it to govern the proper way to increase in value. And so when we look at who is accountable for that, it's the board of directors. The board of directors are there to govern or to at least direct how governance is done. within the organization so that ultimately the organization itself increases in value. Now the board of directors are typically not in the best position to oversee what the actual company does. and so they appoint a CEO that becomes directly accountable for what we call corporate governance. So corporate governance are all of those activities, initiatives, undertakings that the organization does in order to really increase its value and the value of its assets. So what does a company do for example as part of corporate governance? puts out new products, it acquires other companies, it improves how it does certain processes and functions within the organization, it strikes relationships with other companies, for example, all focused on increasing the value of the organization itself. Now what security does is called security governance. And so what are these? These are all of the activities, the initiatives, the undertakings that the security function drives and supports within the organization. And so what security does is called security governance. It's also called security management, which is the focus of this entire domain. And so what security does should be aligned and focused on corporate governance activities. So if you think about all of those things that the organization does, that are ultimately there to allow it to achieve its goals and objectives, that are always focused on increasing the value of the organization itself. Why wouldn't security be aligned with that? and contributing towards that and focusing and aligned with all of those activities that are focused on increasing value. I mean value is obviously a very important word in any organization. And it doesn't matter what industry you're in, what your goals and objectives are, any organization is always gonna be focused on this and increasing it. And so the CEO is directly accountable for driving corporate governance that ultimately is focused on increasing the value of the organization. Security has to be aligned with that. Why would any organization want what security does to go away from that or to take away from that? And so if you think about how organizations will establish and assign budgets and resources to the security function, You want that to return governance, corporate governance, so that we're focused on increasing value. The only way that this works is to align the two. But the two can only be aligned top down. And that is, you cannot just establish, for example, budgets and resources to security and hope that security does the right things that will ultimately focus on corporate governance. Security professionals aren't really focused on all of those activities that the corporate governance and those that are accountable for it are focused on. So the alignment has to be top-down. And that means that those that are accountable for corporate governance, the CEO, the board of directors, and other high-level executives, need to drive what security does in its activities and initiatives and undertakings. It cannot work the other way around. The two need to be aligned, but it has to be a top down. And this is where companies fail and security functions fail. When this alignment does not exist, but it's not driven top down. It has to be driven top down. And the way that we ensure this is by involving those people that are focused on corporate governance activities to drive what security does. And so that usually requires the establishment of a corporate governance committee, security governance committee that drives what security does. And what they do is they meet on a regular basis and they will prioritize and initiate and justify, for example, all of those initiatives that the security function is going to undertake that are ultimately going to be aligned with corporate governance activities. It's the only way that can work. And based on the importance of what security does today through its security management, it has to be established this way it has to be aligned this way and we're going to show you a slide that really shows the alignment and how important it is in how we do this in most organizations today so the answer to the question that you see up on the board is how do we align security with what's really important to the organization itself is top down, but it has to involve those people that are ultimately accountable for meeting the goals and objectives of the organization itself. And so what you see on your slide here is that those that are accountable, including the CEO, need to become accountable for what security does or does not do. And the only way that that can happen is for them to be directly involved and have that conviction that is required. in order for security to do the things that it needs in order to allow the organization to increase in value. What you see on this slide is sort of a structure that a lot of organizations try and get to to ensure that alignment that is top-down focused on goals and objectives of the organization itself. What you see at the top is the most important. and it's called Corporate Governance and Executive Oversight. And maybe the word oversight is not important enough to use there. It should probably say something like conviction. But what it means is upper management and the executive understands and believes very strongly the importance of security contributing towards the goals and objectives of the organization and because of that they end up being not only involved but also committed and convicted about how important it is that security contributes to those goals and objectives. for example compliance there is no way that compliance can be achieved unless the compliance requirements are actually driven top down as we've said before and then everything else that you see in that slide is what's required but the bottom line is that everything has to be top down like for example the next piece that you see there is security organization and what that means is where is actually security function placed in the organization and who does it report to. And because it's directly related to and contributing towards goals and objectives, the security function should report to the CEO and not, for example, the CIO who's only focused on protecting information. That doesn't work anymore. It has to be related to and contributing towards what the CEO is accountable for and then when you get to the bottom for example here you've got security solutions like identity management and physical security and network security and some of the other pieces that are part of what we call a security program. But look how we got to those. We got to those top down, based on commitment, involvement, conviction. from upper management because they understand and believe the importance of security and how it needs to contribute towards all of those things that equate to value of the organization, which we call corporate governance initiatives. But what security does needs to ultimately be aligned with those. But the only way that that alignment works is top down by involving those that are accountable for goals and objectives of the organization.
